text: The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the liver to expose the anterior wall of the choledochal cyst, carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue to reveal the cyst structure.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection, carefully separating the cyst from surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the upper neck of the choledochal cyst and performs blunt dissection of the posterior wall, carefully separating it from the adjacent portal vein and hepatic artery.
The surgeon places a vessel loop around the upper neck of the choledochal cyst, which has been previously dissected and individualized from surrounding structures.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the cyst, progressively separating it from surrounding tissues as it is mobilized down towards the pancreas.
The surgeon identifies the lower neck of the cyst located in the extra-pancreatic portion of the common bile duct, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon partially opens the cyst, revealing the biliary sludge outlet and a plastic stent, which is then removed from the surgical field.
The surgeon transfixes the lower neck of the cyst using a poly-dianone thread, reinforced with two hemologs, securing the structure in place.
The surgeon performs a distal section between the previously dissected upper and lower necks of the cyst, separating these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon applies anterior traction to expose the posterior wall of the cyst, carefully dissecting the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon encounters adhesions and local inflammation in the surgical field, complicating the maneuver due to a previous cholecystectomy.
The surgeon achieves adequate exposure of the surgical field, ensuring the hepatic pedicle remains intact and undisturbed.
The surgeon performs a sharp transection at the level of proximal resection, located one centimeter below the hepatic duct confluence.
The surgeon places the entire cyst into a plastic bag for removal, ensuring containment of the specimen.
Biliary stones spontaneously flow out from the proximal biliary tree, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs colegoclisis, a procedure involving the removal or bypass of the gallbladder, amidst adhesions and local inflammation from a previous cholecystectomy.
The surgeon identifies the point for division of the proximal jejunum, located sixty centimeters from the angle of traits, in preparation for creating the root limb.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapler to divide the small bowel at the identified location, completing the transection of the proximal jejunum.

The surgeon uses an ultrasonic device to divide the mesentery towards its base, followed by creating an enterotomy on the anti-mesenteric side of the root limb, positioned four centimeters from the stapled end.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side anastomosis by taking full-thickness bites of the duct and jejunum, using a single-layer, interrupted suture technique.
The surgeon uses a non-absorbable monofilament suture to secure the anastomosis, ensuring the connection between the duct and jejunum is stable.
The surgeon creates a suture row by stitching inside-out on the bowel and outside-in on the bile duct, forming a connection between these structures.
The surgeon secures the completed suture to surrounding structures to prevent tension on the anastomosis site.
The surgeon creates a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy between the alimentary and biliary limb, positioned four centimeters distal to the previously performed end-to-side anastomosis.
The surgeon creates small adjacent enterotomies in each limb of the jejuno-jejunostomy, preparing the site for anastomosis.
The surgeon introduces a vascular staple load into the enterotomy, ensuring that the mesentery is not included between the lines of suture.
The surgeon closes the common enterostomy using a running suture with 4-0 polydioxanone, ensuring the closure is secure and continuous.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using interwrapped non-absorbable sutures, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
